item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why I've only translated controversial articles. But I like it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: The Spanko reference actually was a coincidence, and I have something planned for him so I'd rather not have that link there; other than that, the links are fine.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below, with author request accounted for. The author may revert at any time.

+ show block
– hide block

Alright, quite a few neat little references in this one. First off, the most obvious. Given the number of this skip and the fact a character is called Spanko in it, I don't think its coincidence and a reference to everyone's favorite corncrake, SCP-2337. Additionally, Lusia is mentioned as being a pretty big deal in About Deer College, so a reference there is not out of line. Finally, there are mentions of the GOC and the AO list. As such, I am suggesting the following:

Editing this:

AKA "Franco Spanko", a producer of anomalous pornography released from Foundation custody in 1993 after amnestic treatment.

To this:

AKA "Franco Spanko", a producer of anomalous pornography released from Foundation custody in 1993 after amnestic treatment.

Editing this:

See GOC casefile LTE-5414-Warhol: "Carnivorous Photograph" for further information.

To this:

See GOC casefile LTE-5414-Warhol: "Carnivorous Photograph" for further information.

Editing this:

See Anomalous Object 37219.

To this:

See Anomalous Object 37219.

Editing this:

While Bellocchio was never acknowledged by the mundane art world, she achieved some notoriety in the anomalous art community for her photography series

To this:

While Bellocchio was never acknowledged by the mundane art world, she achieved some notoriety in the anomalous art community for her photography series

item.title: also...
item.content:encoded: A other way of looking at it is a loss of creativity. The Artist is sad not because these photos keep eating people but that eating people is all they can do. There is nothing new they can do and the artist can't make anything other than cannibal pics. So, she tries something new. Performance art. Over and over again…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, tho I feel like it needs something more to tie together thematically. How is she helping anything or making amends thru this display?

Also, if the brand of her clothing is random, why is it mentioned. This stuck out very strange to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Between the name and the SCP number, that's quite the shout-out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't know what to think of this piece, so I kind of ignored it after it came out, but psul put into words what I couldn't. We know a lot about AWCY killing people with art, and the consequences of that, but how do they GET to that point? Tell us a story we haven't heard yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed entirely with both of the comments above, other than that I downvoted.

I can understand why someone might want to stop making murderous art. Explain to me why someone would start in the first place, and I might be more interested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This kind of feedback isn't helpful to the author, even if it is positive i think?

Please, be more specific and elaborate why you like this article. This way, an author can learn what their readers might like to see from them in the future. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this - there's a lot here I like, it's a cool anomaly overall, but then I get to the backstory and it just jams me up. I tend to dislike AWCY in general because their "this murder is Art" schtick seems desperately thin to me to begin with, so for one of these people who made a career out of random murder to then randomly feel bad about it only to channel that back into more violent artwork… It just leaves me with a feeling of "OK, whatever."

I still dig the basic idea (and would probably have upvoted if the remorseful-anartist angle hadn't been there) and it's well written so I ended up at neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: well it sure spooked me +1 vote for spookyness

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. The pacing is good, and I had a pang of sypathy and horror at "human remains on concrete". That said, I feel the third footnote spells out the meaning a bit too much, and the seventh could be removed without detriment to the article. Still a solid piece overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't say I'm a fan. Bellocchio's characterization really isn't strong here. Like, are we supposed to be like, "Oh dear, the poor mass murderer and friend of other murderers killed herself?" That's not a compelling narrative to me. The idea that she'd become desperate does show through a little bit with the last line of her note, but otherwise, all we're treated to is a lady jumping off a balcony, repeatedly, on the anniversary of her suicide, for 30+ years.

I wrote an article that's a LOT like this, so perhaps I'm biased but I don't think what this has to say about depression and suicide and art is really substantial. I don't feel bad for her at all. She says it herself: she's killed a ton of people and justified it as art. I can't sympathize with her in the slightest. I'd rather see a story where she redeems herself, or tries to redeem herself and utterly fails. We don't get that.

Neutral vote for now, leaning towards downvote.
